examples.name: bubble_sort
examples.title: Bubble sort
examples.desc: Real sliding swaps; array + compare + swap.

examples.name: two_pointer
examples.title: Two pointer
examples.desc: lo/hi index carets scanning inward on a sorted array.

examples.name: stack_queue
examples.title: Stack queue
examples.desc: LIFO stack + FIFO queue, with action-point carets.

examples.name: linked_list
examples.title: Linked list
examples.desc: Singly / doubly / circular — classic node anatomy + pointer re-threading.

examples.name: hashmap
examples.title: Hashmap
examples.desc: Hash a key to a bucket; collisions chain on (separate chaining).

examples.name: graph
examples.title: Graph
examples.desc: Labelled nodes + edges, drawn on; reflowing links.

examples.name: graph_moving
examples.title: Graph moving
examples.desc: Drag a vertex and its incident edges follow.

examples.name: bfs_dfs
examples.title: Bfs dfs
examples.desc: The same graph, queue vs stack, with live frontier readouts.

examples.name: dijkstra
examples.title: Dijkstra
examples.desc: Weighted edges, settling distances, a shortest-path tree.

examples.name: calculus-demo
examples.title: Calculus demo
examples.desc: The flagship: two big ideas on one curve.

examples.name: sine_wave
examples.title: Sine wave
examples.desc: axes + plot, a curve traced on, then vectors.

examples.name: function_graph
examples.title: Function graph
examples.desc: Plot an expression straight from a formula string.

examples.name: area_under_curve
examples.title: Area under curve
examples.desc: Riemann rectangles sweeping to the integral.

examples.name: riemann_rainbow
examples.title: Riemann rainbow
examples.desc: Coloured Riemann rectangles revealed one by one.

examples.name: riemann_readout
examples.title: Riemann readout
examples.desc: Running sums shown as a live computed number.

examples.name: tangent
examples.title: Tangent
examples.desc: The tangent line to a curve, sliding along it — its tilt is read from the function itself, so it's always the true slope (flat at the peaks).

examples.name: analysis
examples.title: Analysis
examples.desc: Ask one curve everything at once — tangent, a live slope number, the normal, the area sweeping open beneath it, and the integral climbing to its true value.

examples.name: newton
examples.title: Newton
examples.desc: Newton's method, drawn as a zig-zag: from a first guess, slide down each tangent to the axis, back up to the curve, and watch the guesses walk to the root.

examples.name: inverse-derivatives
examples.title: Inverse derivatives
examples.desc: Why a function and its inverse have reciprocal slopes: e^x and ln x mirrored across y = x, with the slopes at matching points multiplying to 1.

examples.name: spline
examples.title: Spline
examples.desc: Interpolation: one smooth curve drawn through a scattered set of points — it passes through every knot exactly.

examples.name: trajectory
examples.title: Trajectory
examples.desc: A phase portrait: three paths flowing under a differential system, each spiralling into the sink at the origin.

examples.name: linear-algebra
examples.title: Linear algebra
examples.desc: A guided lesson, not a feature demo: five chapters that build linear algebra as one connected story.

examples.name: linear-map
examples.title: Linear map
examples.desc: What a 2×2 matrix does to space: the grid deforms and the basis lands on its columns (linmap), the unit square's area becomes the determinant (determinant), and two directions only stretch — the eigenvectors (eigen).

examples.name: linear-system
examples.title: Linear system
examples.desc: The geometry of solving and spanning, in three panels: a 2×2 system as two lines crossing at the solution (linsolve), two independent vectors reaching the whole plane, and two parallel vectors collapsing to a line — rank 1 (span).

examples.name: diagonalise
examples.title: Diagonalise
examples.desc: A = P D P⁻¹ made visual: every real-diagonalisable matrix has a basis — its eigenvectors — in which it does nothing but *stretch* each axis.

examples.name: rref
examples.title: Rref
examples.desc: Gaussian elimination, animated: an augmented matrix [A | b] is reduced to reduced row-echelon form one row operation at a time, the numbers transforming in place until the left block is the identity and the last column is the solution (rref).

examples.name: projection
examples.title: Projection
examples.desc: One idea, two faces: orthogonal **projection** drops a vector onto a subspace (the shadow is the closest point, the error meets the space at a right angle), and **least-squares** fits a line to data the same way — minimising the squared residuals (project, leastsquares).

examples.name: matrix
examples.title: Matrix
examples.desc: A bracketed matrix, rows/columns addressable via tags.

examples.name: matrix_addition
examples.title: Matrix addition
examples.desc: Two matrices summed, cell by cell.

examples.name: matrix_addition_plane
examples.title: Matrix addition plane
examples.desc: The same sum, laid out on a coordinate plane.

examples.name: linear_transform
examples.title: Linear transform
examples.desc: A 2x2 matrix shearing a grid + basis vectors.

examples.name: table
examples.title: Table
examples.desc: A ruled table; cells, rows, columns, labels all addressable.

examples.name: table_braces
examples.title: Table braces
examples.desc: A table annotated with braces.

examples.name: statistics
examples.title: Statistics
examples.desc: A guided lesson, not a feature demo: describe a dataset, meet the normal curve, then see *why* the bell is everywhere (the Central Limit Theorem).

examples.name: histogram
examples.title: Histogram
examples.desc: The shape of a dataset: a list of numbers binned into bars that stagger in one at a time, with the mean marked and the range labelled (histogram).

examples.name: summary
examples.title: Summary
examples.desc: Describe a dataset in one call: the numbers as dots on a number line, with the mean, median and mode marked, a ±1σ spread band, and readouts of the range, variance and standard deviation (summary).

examples.name: boxplot
examples.title: Boxplot
examples.desc: The five-number summary as a box-and-whisker: the box spans Q1→Q3 (its width is the interquartile range), a line marks the median, the whiskers reach the rest, and a value far outside is flagged as an outlier (boxplot).

examples.name: skew
examples.title: Skew
examples.desc: Which way does the tail point? A histogram with the mean and median marked and a labelled skewness — when the mean is dragged right of the median, the data is right-skewed (skew).

examples.name: bellcurve
examples.title: Bellcurve
examples.desc: The normal (Gaussian) bell curve and the 68-95-99.7 rule: the bell draws in, then the ±1σ / ±2σ / ±3σ bands shade one at a time, showing that 68% of values fall within one standard deviation, 95% within two, and 99.7% within three (bellcurve, alias gaussian).

examples.name: clt
examples.title: Clt
examples.desc: The Central Limit Theorem — the flagship: however flat a single die is, the *average* of five dice, taken 1200 times, piles into a bell that hugs the normal curve (clt).

examples.name: correlation
examples.title: Correlation
examples.desc: Do two things move together? The scatter of paired data, the best-fit line, and the Pearson correlation r — near +1 a tight upward line, near −1 downward, near 0 a shapeless blob (correlation).

examples.name: lln
examples.title: Lln
examples.desc: The Law of Large Numbers: flip a fair coin over and over and track the running proportion of heads.

examples.name: hypothesis
examples.title: Hypothesis
examples.desc: Is a result surprising enough to be real? Under the null hypothesis the test statistic follows the standard normal; the observed z cuts off tails whose area is the p-value.

examples.name: covariance
examples.title: Covariance
examples.desc: Covariance as signed area: a cross at the means, and a rectangle from each point to the centre — cyan where x and y agree, magenta where they disagree.

examples.name: bayes
examples.title: Bayes
examples.desc: Bayesian updating: a prior belief about a coin's bias, the likelihood from the data, and the posterior that combines them — pulled toward the evidence and sharpening as it accumulates (bayes).

examples.name: probability
examples.title: Probability
examples.desc: A probability & sampling playground in four chapters: named distributions (uniform / exponential / binomial / Poisson), a confidence interval, a Monte-Carlo estimate of π, and a random walk (distribution, confidence, montecarlo, randomwalk).

examples.name: pendulum
examples.title: Pendulum
examples.desc: One pendulum shown four ways from a single deterministic swing: the motion (with a velocity arrow + KE/PE bars), the phase portrait (θ vs ω), a time series, the potential-energy well, and energy over time (pendulum + phase/timegraph/ well/energygraph + swing).

examples.name: pendulum-damped
examples.title: Pendulum damped
examples.desc: The same four views with friction on (damping): the swing decays, the phase loop spirals inward, the well ball settles, and the total-energy line drops — dissipation told the same way by every panel.

examples.name: pendulum-annotated
examples.title: Pendulum annotated
examples.desc: A guided anatomy lesson proving physics composes with base manic: section chapters, text / arrow / bracelabel annotations, and show/recolor/flash/pulse all driving the sim's parts — no special physics mode.

examples.name: spring
examples.title: Spring
examples.desc: A mass on a spring (simple harmonic motion) drawn with a real stretching coil — the same generic views on a *different* system; note the energy well is a **parabola** (½kx²) rather than the pendulum's cosine (spring + the views + run).

examples.name: spring-damped
examples.title: Spring damped
examples.desc: The damped spring: the coil's oscillation decays, the phase ellipse spirals in, the ball settles in the parabola, and total energy bleeds away.

examples.name: double-pendulum
examples.title: Double pendulum
examples.desc: Deterministic chaos: two arms hinged end-to-end whose outer bob traces a wild, unrepeatable curve — yet the render is frame-identical every run.

examples.name: spring-pendulum
examples.title: Spring pendulum
examples.desc: An elastic pendulum — a bob on a springy rod (drawn as a stretching coil) that both swings and bounces, energy sloshing between the two modes (springpendulum).

examples.name: kapitza
examples.title: Kapitza
examples.desc: The Kapitza pendulum: vibrate the pivot fast enough and the **inverted** position becomes stable — the bob hovers near the top instead of falling (kapitza).

examples.name: cart-pendulum
examples.title: Cart pendulum
examples.desc: A pendulum on a spring-mounted cart rolling on a track — the classic control-theory system; cart and bob trade momentum and energy (cartpendulum).

examples.name: compare-pendulum
examples.title: Compare pendulum
examples.desc: Sensitive dependence: two identical driven pendulums started 0.001 rad apart drift onto completely different paths — the butterfly effect, watched in phase/timegraph (comparependulum).

examples.name: vertical-spring
examples.title: Vertical spring
examples.desc: A mass bobbing on a vertical spring under gravity — gravity shifts the equilibrium but the energy well stays a parabola (verticalspring).

examples.name: spring-incline
examples.title: Spring incline
examples.desc: A mass on a spring on an inclined plane; gravity's along-ramp component sets a new stretched rest point it oscillates about (springincline).

examples.name: bungee
examples.title: Bungee
examples.desc: A bungee jump: free-fall, then a ONE-SIDED elastic cord (it only pulls) catches and bounces the jumper — note the lopsided energy well (bungee).

examples.name: resonance
examples.title: Resonance
examples.desc: A driven spring pushed near its natural frequency √(k/m): the amplitude climbs and climbs — resonance, watched building up in phase/energygraph (resonance).

examples.name: double-spring
examples.title: Double spring
examples.desc: Two masses coupled by springs between walls — push one and the energy sloshes back and forth (beating); normal modes show as diagonals in phase (doublespring).

examples.name: series-parallel-springs
examples.title: Series parallel springs
examples.desc: The same mass on springs in series (soft, slow) vs parallel (stiff, fast), side by side — the timegraph makes the frequency difference obvious (seriesparallel).

examples.name: car-suspension
examples.title: Car suspension
examples.desc: A quarter-car riding a scrolling road — a speed bump, a washboard stretch, and a pothole — its spring+damper soaking up the ride (carsuspension).

examples.name: vector_field
examples.title: Vector field
examples.desc: A magnitude-coloured vector field.

examples.name: coordinates
examples.title: Coordinates
examples.desc: Axes, planes, number lines, polar & complex planes.

examples.name: pie
examples.title: Pie
examples.desc: A pie chart built from sectors.

examples.name: equilateral
examples.title: Equilateral
examples.desc: Euclid I.1 — an equilateral triangle from two circles.

examples.name: triangle
examples.title: Triangle
examples.desc: A triangle with its centres and cevians.

examples.name: incircle_tangents
examples.title: Incircle tangents
examples.desc: The incircle and its tangent points.

examples.name: tangents
examples.title: Tangents
examples.desc: Tangent lines from a point to a circle.

examples.name: orthocenter
examples.title: Orthocenter
examples.desc: The orthocentre from the three altitudes.

examples.name: euler_line
examples.title: Euler line
examples.desc: The Euler line through centroid, circumcentre, orthocentre.

examples.name: nine_point
examples.title: Nine point
examples.desc: The nine-point circle.

examples.name: conics
examples.title: Conics
examples.desc: Ellipse, parabola, hyperbola.

examples.name: transforms
examples.title: Transforms
examples.desc: Apply a 2x2 matrix (ApplyMatrix) to a group.

examples.name: transform_copy
examples.title: Transform copy
examples.desc: Duplicate an entity, then transform the copy.

examples.name: morph
examples.title: Morph
examples.desc: A sampled-point shape morph from A to B.

examples.name: typewriter
examples.title: Typewriter
examples.desc: Text revealed character by character.

examples.name: captions
examples.title: Captions
examples.desc: Karaoke / word-pop caption modes.

examples.name: terminal_boot
examples.title: Terminal boot
examples.desc: The neon terminal template booting up.

examples.name: brace
examples.title: Brace
examples.desc: The curly-brace family.

examples.name: banner
examples.title: Banner
examples.desc: The manic logo / banner reveal.

examples.name: fractal_tree
examples.title: Fractal tree
examples.desc: One recursive def, drawn to depth 12.

examples.name: hue_wave
examples.title: Hue wave
examples.desc: An animated hue wave across a grid.

examples.name: hill_run
examples.title: Hill run
examples.desc: A little scene animated with the language layer.

examples.name: equal_cuts
examples.title: Equal cuts
examples.desc: A circle halved again and again (pizza cuts).

examples.name: archimedes_pi
examples.title: Archimedes pi
examples.desc: Bounding pi with inscribed / circumscribed polygons.

examples.name: pieday
examples.title: Pieday
examples.desc: A Pi Day card: a rainbow petal-flower built from a loop of circles, radial rays, the digits of π, and the definition circumference / diameter = pi.

examples.name: boolean
examples.title: Boolean
examples.desc: Union / intersection / difference of shapes.

examples.name: three_d
examples.title: Three d
examples.desc: Cubes, spheres, arrows, a curve, a surface and solids together — the 3D basics on one stage.

examples.name: solids3
examples.title: Solids3
examples.desc: Filled, shaded solids: a prism, a cone, and a lathed vase.

examples.name: param3
examples.title: Param3
examples.desc: Parametric surfaces a height field can't make — a torus, a sphere, and a Möbius strip.

examples.name: extrude3
examples.title: Extrude3
examples.desc: Lifting flat shapes into solids, including a boolean cut-out (a plate with a hole) and an L-beam.

examples.name: morph3
examples.title: Morph3
examples.desc: Morphing across families — a cube into a sphere, a saddle into a bowl, a helix into a ring.

examples.name: linear-algebra-3d
examples.title: Linear algebra 3d
examples.desc: The 3D companion to the linear-algebra lesson: one matrix [[1,0,0],[0,3,1],[0,1,3]] (det 8; eigenvalues 1, 2, 4) seen two ways on an orbiting stage — first as a transformation (the unit cube → a parallelepiped whose volume is the determinant), then through its eigenvectors (the invariant axes that only stretch).

examples.name: linear-map3
examples.title: Linear map3
examples.desc: Linear algebra in 3D: a 3×3 matrix deforms the unit cube into a parallelepiped, with basis arrows i/j/k landing on the matrix's columns and the enclosed volume labelled as the determinant (linmap3).

examples.name: eigen3
examples.title: Eigen3
examples.desc: The real eigenvectors of a 3×3 matrix, in 3D: the invariant lines through the origin that only stretch (by λ) when the matrix acts (eigen3).

examples.name: matrix3
examples.title: Matrix3
examples.desc: A 3×3×3 block of cubes, with a shear matrix **M** and its inverse **M⁻¹** applied and undone.

examples.name: double-integral3
examples.title: Double integral3
examples.desc: Multivariable calculus: the volume under a surface as a limit of finer and finer columns — a double integral, made solid.
